topics: Byzantine vs. Roman Empire nomenclature debate
Long-term continuity and identity of the Roman state
Imperial persona, rhetoric, and governance
Periodization and key crisis points of Roman history
Roman law, citizenship, and social cohesion

claims.claim: The Byzantine Empire is a misnomer invented by later historians; the people of that empire always called themselves Romans and it was legally and culturally the Roman Empire continuing unbroken.
evidence: All primary sources refer to the state as the Roman Empire and its subjects as Roman citizens
Western Europeans called it the 'Empire of the Greeks' for political reasons before switching to 'Byzantine Empire'
Kaldellis says 'the burden of proof is on those who would assert that what we've been calling the Byzantine Empire is something other than the Roman Empire'

claims.claim: The Roman state lasted over 2,200 years, from its founding in 753 BC to the fall of Constantinople in 1453 AD, making it nearly unique in history.
evidence: Founding dated to 753 BC, fall of Constantinople to 1453 AD
Kaldellis describes it as 'an extraordinarily long time. Very unique almost in history'

claims.claim: The three principal crisis points that caused rapid, catastrophic territorial losses for the Eastern Roman Empire were the Arab conquests of the 630s, the Seljuk-Turk conquest of Asia Minor in the 1070s, and the Fourth Crusade sack of Constantinople in 1204.
evidence: The Arab conquest was completed in roughly a decade in the 630s
The Seljuk-Turk conquest of Asia Minor (modern Turkey) occurred in the 1070s
The Fourth Crusade in 1204, led mostly by French armies, sacked and dismembered the empire

claims.claim: The Eastern Roman emperors consistently projected a persona of tireless, selfless, responsive governance—and Kaldellis's research suggests they generally acted in accordance with this rhetoric.
evidence: Emperors used laws, imperial rhetoric, and petitions to broadcast that they governed solely for subjects' benefit
Justinian was publicly known as the 'sleepless emperor' for his workaholic governing style
Kaldellis states 'I have found that they generally did what they said they were going to do overall'

claims.claim: The small gap between imperial rhetoric and action in the Eastern Roman Empire was partly explained by the emperors' vulnerability: there was no hereditary right to the throne, incentivizing them to maintain subject consent.
evidence: Kaldellis states 'there is no right to the throne' as a key factor
Emperors 'needed their subjects to be relatively content'
The consensus to be taxed and ruled depended on subjects believing emperors were fulfilling their responsibilities

claims.claim: Roman law provided women and minorities within the empire with legal rights that were superior to those in their own religious communities, illustrated by Jewish women preferring Roman courts over rabbinical ones in the 10th–11th centuries.
evidence: Jewish women in the Eastern Roman Empire had more rights in Roman courts than in rabbinical courts
Roman courts treated these as property matters rather than religious matters, giving Roman jurisdiction precedence

claims.claim: The Roman Empire's history is best understood as the history of a specific political community (state), not a culture or religion, and its continuity is analogous to the Ship of Theseus—all components change gradually but the narrative identity persists.
evidence: Kaldellis contrasts Roman history with Greek culture or Christian history, which can exist across different states and ethnic groups
He states 'Roman history is very specifically the history of a state or a political community'
The Ship of Theseus metaphor: 'it's the narrative that you tell about it where the changes that take place to it make sense only when you see them within the context of that story'

claims.claim: Conventional historical dates like 395 AD (East-West split) and 476 AD (fall of the West) are shorthand for long processes, not discrete events.
evidence: The 395 split was 'one among many allocations of jurisdiction' with subsequent reunifications
The fall of the Western Empire in 476 'is actually a very long process that took decades'
Kaldellis: 'sometimes we're talking about processes, sometimes we're talking about very discrete events'

quote_candidates.text: the burden of proof is on those who would assert that what we've been calling the Byzantine Empire is something other than the Roman Empire. Because all of our sources are very clear about this.
quote_candidates.speaker: Anthony Kaldellis

quote_candidates.text: It's almost a form of cognitive dissonance, right? It's like when you know something is the case, but you carry on as if it's not.
quote_candidates.speaker: Anthony Kaldellis

quote_candidates.text: you're making a deal here with the Romans, the Romans, the name should say it all.
quote_candidates.speaker: Anthony Kaldellis

quote_candidates.text: Roman history is very specifically the history of a state or a political community.
quote_candidates.speaker: Anthony Kaldellis

quote_candidates.text: it's the narrative that you tell about it where the changes that take place to it make sense only when you see them within the context of that story.
quote_candidates.speaker: Anthony Kaldellis

quote_candidates.text: the emperors are constantly saying that we're planning ahead. We're trying to foresee what problems you might have and we are solving them before you experience them.
quote_candidates.speaker: Anthony Kaldellis

guest_candidates.name: Anthony Kaldellis
guest_candidates.title: Historian of the Eastern Roman Empire / Byzantine Empire

topics: Roman imperial legitimacy and civil war dynamics
Edict of Caracalla and Roman citizenship expansion
Crisis of the Third Century and Diocletian's reforms
The Hippodrome as political barometer in Constantinople
Taxation as the foundation of the Eastern Roman Empire

claims.claim: Approximately 46% of emperors of Constantinople were overthrown through violence, making every emperor perpetually insecure.
evidence: 'Something like forty six percent of the emperors of Constantinople are overthrown through violence'
This created an ongoing incentive for emperors to maintain popular goodwill rather than rule by fear

claims.claim: The Hippodrome functioned as a de facto ongoing referendum on imperial legitimacy, where crowd reactions served as real-time opinion polls for emperors.
evidence: 100,000 people could gather in the Hippodrome to chant acclamations or boo emperors
Emperor Alexius III publicly reversed the 'German tax' after crowd uproar in the Hippodrome in the 1190s
Tepid or absent cheering signaled policy problems emperors needed to address

claims.claim: The Edict of Caracalla (212 AD) was uniquely implemented in practice, unlike other empires' citizenship extensions, enabling provincials to hold the highest offices within a generation.
evidence: Within a generation of the edict, all emperors were provincial
Explosion of people named 'Aurelius' across provinces confirms immediate uptake
Contrasted with the British Empire never meaningfully extending citizenship to Indians

claims.claim: The crisis of the Third Century saw 26 emperors murdered in 50 years, compounded by hyperinflation, a plague killing up to 5,000 people per day in Rome alone, and the empire fracturing into three parts.
evidence: 26 emperors in 50 years
Plague from 249 to 262 AD, possibly smallpox or Ebola
Empire fractured into three pieces before Aurelian reconquered them in five years

claims.claim: Diocletian resolved the Third Century crisis by institutionalizing bigger government, universal taxation (including ending Italy's tax exemption), and a universal census, creating the fiscal framework the Eastern Roman Empire used for centuries.
evidence: Italy had been tax-exempt as 'the land of the conquerors' until Diocletian
Universal census catalogued every taxable asset in the empire
Illyrian emperors from the Balkans saw no reason for Italian tax privilege

claims.claim: The Roman civil wars of the Byzantine period were never ideological—all factions shared the same political values and competed solely over who should hold power.
evidence: 'Something like 120 civil wars' in the thousand years Kaldellis studies
'They're never about ideology... They're solely about who has the power. All sides have the same ideology'
Contrasted with modern civil wars fought over slavery or rights of kings

claims.claim: Diocletian's social mobility restrictions (farmers bound to land, soldiers' sons obligated to serve) were primarily tax enforcement mechanisms, and in practice the empire remained a society of high physical mobility.
evidence: Farmers were fiscally responsible for land taxes but not physically prevented from leaving
No recorded complaints of unwilling sons forced into military service
Shipping guilds' hereditary obligations tied to asset transfer, not coercion

quote_candidates.text: Something like forty six percent of the emperors of Constantinople are overthrown through violence. Forty six percent. That's almost half.
quote_candidates.speaker: Anthony Kaldellis

quote_candidates.text: We don't have those kinds of institutions. We have instead an ongoing referendum. We just call it a perpetual referendum.
quote_candidates.speaker: Anthony Kaldellis

quote_candidates.text: No modern empire has ever done this, really. What's significant about the Roman case is that not only did they extend citizenship to everybody, but they meant it.
quote_candidates.speaker: Anthony Kaldellis

quote_candidates.text: The problem is the land who owns it that owns the people.
quote_candidates.speaker: Anthony Kaldellis

quote_candidates.text: If you asked me to put my finger on one factor, it would be that. Taxation is the heart of it.
quote_candidates.speaker: Anthony Kaldellis

quote_candidates.text: He turns the problem into its own solution. The problem is too many emperors... So what he does is he deputizes some of his colleagues.
quote_candidates.speaker: Anthony Kaldellis

guest_candidates.name: Anthony Kaldellis
guest_candidates.title: Historian of Byzantine/Eastern Roman Empire, University (specialist in Byzantine studies)

topics: Constantine's rise to power and founding of Constantinople
Christianity's adoption and spread in the Roman Empire
Strategic importance of Constantinople's geography
Roman, Christian, and Greek identity in the Eastern Empire
Diocletian's stabilization and succession crisis

claims.claim: Diocletian stabilized the empire by installing four co-emperors with four armies, reducing rebellions by distributed military authority, and by defeating the Persians, enabling economic recovery.
evidence: Aurelian had already united the empire territorially before Diocletian
Galerius beat the Persians under the Diocletianic system
Stability created conditions for land reclamation and expanded production

claims.claim: Constantine emerged as sole Roman emperor in 324 AD after a series of civil wars against rivals who were often his own in-laws, defeating his last rival Licinius at Byzantium.
evidence: Constantine defeats Licinius in 324 at Byzantium on the Bosporus
Rivals included father-in-laws and brother-in-laws through dynastic intermarriage
The Diocletianic system was designed to be non-hereditary but broke down into civil war

claims.claim: Constantinople's location between the Danube and Euphrates frontiers and at the junction of Europe and Asia made it strategically superior to Rome for frontier-era emperors.
evidence: Almost all emperors who had a choice preferred the east; only Valentinian chose the west
Constantinople functioned as a clamp unifying the Balkans, Asia Minor, and Syria
After Constantinople's founding, the empire's breaking point shifted from the Bosporus to the Adriatic

claims.claim: Christians represented at most around 10% of the Roman Empire's population under Constantine, and a solidly Christian society (non-Christians ~10-15%) was not achieved until the early 6th century.
evidence: Kaldellis estimates maybe 10% Christian at Constantine's time, calling even that 'possibly very high'
Majority Christian society not achievable until maybe early-to-mid 5th century
Solidly Christian society (non-Christians ~10-15%) reached early 6th century—about five centuries after Jesus

claims.claim: Constantine's conversion to Christianity is better explained by personal religious belief than political calculation, because no convincing political argument for the decision has been identified.
evidence: Christians were not politically powerful elites at the time
Bishops were socially useful but could not deliver clear political gain sufficient to justify 'going full Christian'
Constantine had a pattern of religious visions linked to policy shifts, suggesting genuine spiritual orientation

claims.claim: The Eastern Roman Empire is best understood as a coexistence of three distinct but overlapping identities—Roman, Christian, and Greek—rather than Christianity subsuming the others.
evidence: Romans knew Christians who were not Roman (Bulgarians, Rus) and Romans who were not Christian (pre-Constantine martyred Christians)
Kaldellis finds individuals who go 'all in' on one identity (Roman politics, monasticism, or Greek literature) independently
Kaldellis describes it as 'the only society in history' that is simultaneously Roman, Christian, and Greek in different configurations

claims.claim: Constantinople grew from roughly 25,000 people (ancient Byzantium) to 500,000 in two centuries, a rate only possible through large-scale emigration from the provinces, not natural reproduction.
evidence: 25,000 estimated population of Byzantium when Constantine founded Constantinople
500,000 population reached within two centuries
Natural demographic growth in an ancient city cannot account for that rate

claims.claim: Christianity spread through the empire via incentives (church charity funds, social support networks) and disincentives (legal restrictions on traditional religion, exclusion from offices), not primarily through theological persuasion alone.
evidence: Bishops given imperial funds to distribute charity to communities
Laws progressively restricted animal sacrifice, votive practices, and access to sacred trees
Non-Christians faced problems with wills and exclusion from high offices

quote_candidates.text: Christianity triumphs over the ancient religions, right? So displacing Zeus and Apollo, and all of that, and animal sacrifice is gone. And like, that's because Christianity triumphs. And that's a very narrow way of looking at it.
quote_candidates.speaker: Anthony Kaldellis

quote_candidates.text: Rome capture Christianity? Right? Yes. Which, which, which is the which is the mechanism? I would lean more toward the second, in other words, that the religion was co opted by the imperial system.
quote_candidates.speaker: Anthony Kaldellis

quote_candidates.text: Islam comes into existence, without a pre existing state to receive it and take it on board, it creates its own state, right? So the Muslim armies, they conquer this empire, and then they have to sort of govern it.
quote_candidates.speaker: Anthony Kaldellis

quote_candidates.text: Constantinople ultimately functions as a kind of clamp that unifies this whole area.
quote_candidates.speaker: Anthony Kaldellis

quote_candidates.text: the Turks will at least respect our identity. Like that's a cost.
quote_candidates.speaker: Anthony Kaldellis

quote_candidates.text: I see the society as this kind of laboratory of these three elements coexisting in these fascinating combinations.
quote_candidates.speaker: Anthony Kaldellis

guest_candidates.name: Anthony Kaldellis
guest_candidates.title: Historian of the Byzantine/Eastern Roman Empire, University of Chicago (implied by context)

topics: Fall of the Western Roman Empire
East Roman (Byzantine) governmental structure
Role of eunuchs in Byzantine court
Justinian and his reign
Monarchic republic concept and terminology

claims.claim: Ancient cities functioned as demographic 'death traps,' losing 1-3% of their population per year to disease, requiring constant immigration to maintain stable populations.
evidence: Constantinople required large-scale immigration from Greek-speaking provinces (Greece, Asia Minor) to grow its population
17th-century London cited as an even worse example of urban disease density

claims.claim: Constantinople's founding deliberately diverted resources—specifically grain from Egypt—away from Rome, signaling a permanent eastward shift in imperial priorities.
evidence: Constantine established a grain dole for up to 200,000 people in Constantinople, funded by Egyptian grain previously destined for Rome
Rome redirected to North African and Sicilian grain supplies as a result
Some senators relocated from Rome to Constantinople in response

claims.claim: The fall of the Western Roman Empire was driven by a vicious cycle in which barbarian seizure of provinces (especially North Africa by the Vandals) stripped Rome of tax revenues needed to fund armies to resist further invasions.
evidence: Loss of North Africa's tax revenues made it impossible to mobilize armies against subsequent waves
Goths crossed the Danube in 376; the Battle of Adrianople resulted in Emperor Valens's death and roughly one and a half field armies annihilated
For the first time, Rome accepted a quasi-independent Gothic army inside imperial territory

claims.claim: The Eastern Roman Empire survived partly because geography funneled western invaders away from Asia Minor toward Italy, while Constantinople's fortifications allowed the East to alternate drawing on European and Asian provinces.
evidence: Without a fleet, Goths could not cross from Thrace to Asia Minor, so they eventually moved to Italy ('it's kind of nicer in Naples')
Persian or Arab invaders from the east could not cross into Balkan provinces, allowing the empire to fall back on either side
Constantinople described as an 'impregnable bunker' serving as a stable command center

claims.claim: The East Roman state had no isolated rural communities outside its institutional reach; every parcel of arable land was censused, taxed up to three times a year, and integrated through coins, church, and calendar.
evidence: Tax census officials reached virtually all settled land; literary descriptions of hermits define isolation as where 'not even the tax census people showed up'
Taxes included coin, payment in kind, services, and military recruits
Churches throughout the empire provided calendar, time-keeping, and prayers for the emperor

claims.claim: Eunuchs were strategically valuable to emperors precisely because their lack of family networks and dynastic ambitions made them reliably loyal, and emperors used them as parallel power tracks to counterbalance established military and bureaucratic networks.
evidence: Eunuchs often originated outside the empire or were former slaves with no connections to powerful family networks
Narses, Justinian's eunuch general, defeated the Goths in Italy despite being elderly and physically unimposing
Some Paphlagonian families voluntarily castrated sons as a deliberate court-placement strategy

claims.claim: The East Roman Empire is more accurately described as a 'monarchic republic' than an empire, since its own language lacked a term for empire and its ideology held that the emperor existed to serve the polity (res publica/politia).
evidence: Greek sources use 'vasilia' (monarchy) or 'politia' (polity/res publica), not a term equivalent to 'empire'
The official name of the state was 'Romania,' not 'empire'
Unlike dynastic empires, the emperor was consistently described as serving the republic

claims.claim: The Eastern Roman Empire was not a military dictatorship because the army was almost never used as an instrument of domestic social control across its entire thousand-year history.
evidence: Soldiers were recruited from local villages and lived among the civilian population
Army's role was understood as protecting the Roman people, not suppressing them
Contrast drawn with modern authoritarian regimes (e.g., Mubarak's Egypt during the Arab Spring)

quote_candidates.text: new Rome is literally stealing food from the mouth of old Rome
quote_candidates.speaker: Anthony Kaldellis

quote_candidates.text: It sends a signal about the priorities of the state, like they're investing much more in the East and that the East would be regarded as a equal parallel Roman world.
quote_candidates.speaker: Anthony Kaldellis

quote_candidates.text: The crux of the matter is that barbarian armies, so armies from outside the empire, put increasing pressure on it during the 5th century.
quote_candidates.speaker: Anthony Kaldellis

quote_candidates.text: If you fortified Constantinople so that it's an impregnable bunker, which it was, right? So you're holding your command center in Constantinople. You can alternate between your European or your Asian provinces, depending on where the trouble is.
quote_candidates.speaker: Anthony Kaldellis

quote_candidates.text: Ultimately, I think it's the decision made by local communities to stick with the Roman state. Like ultimately, like they're not going to actively side with invaders. And when the trouble passes, they restore ties with Constantinople immediately. Like this is their natural political home.
quote_candidates.speaker: Anthony Kaldellis

quote_candidates.text: There are wolves roaming the streets of the forum. So this is a complete overhaul of the centers of power. It's turned inside out almost.
quote_candidates.speaker: Anthony Kaldellis

topics: Justinian's legal codification and legacy
Roman-Persian wars and Heraclius
Arab conquests and Byzantine survival
Byzantine military technology (Greek fire)
11th-century Byzantine crisis and external threats

claims.claim: Justinian's Corpus Juris Civilis remains the foundation of Roman law study today and directly influences civil law systems across Europe.
evidence: The codification was led by jurist Trebonian under Justinian's commission
In many European countries, Roman law derived from this corpus is current law
Legal scholars and law students still study Roman law, except in common law countries like the US and Britain

claims.claim: Justinian's wars of conquest overextended the empire and left his successors in a more vulnerable strategic position.
evidence: He disbanded armies protecting Constantinople and sent them to Italy
Persians raided and destroyed Antioch while eastern defenses were weakened
Italy and southern Spain were likely not economically self-sustaining after conquest

claims.claim: The Plague of Justinian (541 AD) had a much more moderate impact than the maximalist scholarly estimate of 50% mortality.
evidence: Roman military campaigns on four or five fronts continued without pause during the plague
Taxation and courts continued functioning with no narrative evidence of disruption
A 50% mortality would logistically have brought society to a halt, which did not occur

claims.claim: Heraclius is overrated as a hero because his victory over Persia was offset by losing everything to the Arab conquests, and he helped cause the crisis he inherited.
evidence: His rebellion (608–610) diverted Roman armies from the Persian front, gifting major gains to Persia
He lost Syria, Palestine, and Egypt to the Arabs in the 630s–640s
His final victory over Persia relied heavily on Turkish allies from Central Asia

claims.claim: Greek fire was a state secret whose exact composition and deployment mechanism remains debated, but it was decisive in repelling Arab and Viking attacks on Constantinople.
evidence: Used successfully against Arab sieges in the 660s–670s and again in the early 8th century
Used against Viking attacks on Constantinople in the 940s and 1040s
Also existed in hand grenade form—clay containers with a fuse, most effective against siege engines

claims.claim: The 11th-century Byzantine crisis was primarily caused by simultaneous external attacks from three different enemies, not internal moral decay or civilian neglect of the military.
evidence: Three-pronged attack: Seljuk Turks from the east, Pechenegs from the north, Normans in southern Italy
No medieval or ancient state could realistically survive simultaneous attack by three very different enemy types
The traditional 'decline and fall' model biases historians toward seeking internal causes

claims.claim: Iconoclasm was an elite controversy of limited popular interest, not a mass social conflict.
evidence: Only a few emperors, bishops, abbots, and monks—perhaps 100–200 people—were seriously invested in it
Sources never mention popular uprisings demanding icons or their removal
When emperors changed policy, almost everyone fell into line without resistance

claims.claim: The Arab conquests succeeded rapidly because both the Roman and Persian empires were already devastated and depleted by their mutual war.
evidence: The 602–628 Persian war stripped Rome of Syria, Palestine, and Egypt, eliminating key revenue and grain supply
Both empires were low on manpower and resources when the Arabs struck
Arabs attacked from a direction neither empire had prepared to defend

quote_candidates.text: Justinian thought it caused earthquakes.
quote_candidates.speaker: Anthony Kaldellis

quote_candidates.text: In other words, yes, he does beat the Persians and he gets, like, in my view, full credit for that, though he did it with, I think, the help of some Turkish allies from Central Asia. These were, I think, the major component of his success at the very end.
quote_candidates.speaker: Anthony Kaldellis

quote_candidates.text: Like Buridan's ass, I can't decide which which bale of hay to put him on. But there's a grand scale to the good and the bad.
quote_candidates.speaker: Anthony Kaldellis

quote_candidates.text: No state can medieval or ancient can realistically survive the simultaneous attack of three very different kinds of enemies.
quote_candidates.speaker: Anthony Kaldellis

quote_candidates.text: Conquest is not just good in itself. I mean, I know that some rulers in history are called the great simply because they conquered something. But that's not enough.
quote_candidates.speaker: Anthony Kaldellis

quote_candidates.text: This is a bubonic plague, which lasts more or less for two centuries. It comes in, it goes and it's kind of endemic to large parts of the world.
quote_candidates.speaker: Anthony Kaldellis

guest_candidates.name: Anthony Kaldellis
guest_candidates.title: Byzantine historian, author of 'Streams of Gold, Rivers of Blood' and 'The New Roman Empire'

topics: Decline and fall of the East Roman Empire
Sources of Byzantine resilience and internal stability
Rhetoric vs. action in imperial governance
Lessons from Roman history for modern America
Phases of human history and the future of civilization

claims.claim: The decisive decline of the East Roman Empire began between 1300 and 1350, when sources of resilience dried up due to loss of Asia Minor, civil wars, Serbian expansion, and the Black Death.
evidence: By 1300 the empire lost Asia Minor to the Turks, eliminating its dual European-Asian resource base
Civil wars and Serbian expansion followed, then the Black Death
By the 1340s the speaker considers it 'game over'

claims.claim: Tamerlane's defeat of the Turks around 1400 delayed the final fall of Constantinople by roughly 50 years, pushing it to 1453.
evidence: Timur Tamerlane came from Central Asia, trounced the Turks, broke up their empire
The Turks entered a rebuilding cycle, delaying conquest until 1453

claims.claim: The East Roman Empire's internal cohesion was so strong that, absent foreign invasions, it could have lasted another 1000 years.
evidence: No separatist movements among Roman provincials except Bulgaria (which rebelled successfully in 1185 after about two centuries of conquest)
No center ever lost the ability to tax its territories
No widespread peasant uprisings or agricultural rebellions
No warlords carved out independent territory

claims.claim: The East Roman Empire's stability rested on two pillars: authorities persuading subjects they ruled on their behalf (and largely doing so), and a tightly unified Roman-Orthodox identity.
evidence: Subjects knew they were surrounded by non-Roman, non-Christian enemies and did not want to live under their power
Complaining about taxes was tolerated and even functional, signaling a healthy relationship between rulers and ruled

claims.claim: Rhetoric without matching action is insufficient for legitimacy; the most effective imperial rhetoric is credible because rulers actually do what they claim.
evidence: Aristotle's advice to tyrants in the Politics: the best way to seem a benevolent king is to be one
Analogy of two deans using different rhetoric for the same budget cuts, producing different levels of compliance
America's gap between lofty rhetoric and military action cited as a modern failure case (Afghanistan, Iraq)

claims.claim: Human history can be divided into three distinct phases—hunter-gatherer, agricultural, and industrial/technological—with a likely fourth phase ahead.
evidence: Hunter-gatherer period constituted the majority of human history so far
Agricultural revolution created surpluses enabling more complex societies
Current technological phase is modifying the parameters of what human beings can do and possibly be

claims.claim: Crises in Roman history were exogenous shocks, not products of internal decomposition; the empire's default trajectory was endogenous regrowth and stabilization.
evidence: After each crisis the Romans 'figure out how to hold a line, consolidate, regroup, and put their economy and society back on a trajectory of revival'
This happened 'every single time, except the last time'
Nobody came from outside to help them rebuild

quote_candidates.text: Always, every single time, except the last time.
quote_candidates.speaker: Anthony Kaldellis

quote_candidates.text: My answer is unequivocally the latter. So I see this as a society whose internal organization primes it to stabilize and embark on steady growth, slow, and gradual, but steady.
quote_candidates.speaker: Anthony Kaldellis

quote_candidates.text: If there's no significant external invasions, the East Roman Empire, the fundamentals, the taxation system, the way the policies were developed, the way the politics was done, the way the representation was done, all of that, this empire could have lasted for another 1000 years. Absolutely. I'm absolutely convinced of this.
quote_candidates.speaker: Anthony Kaldellis

quote_candidates.text: The best way to persuade your subjects that you're legitimate, benevolent kings is to be that.
quote_candidates.speaker: Anthony Kaldellis

quote_candidates.text: I don't think most of the world even believes what American leaders say their goals are, especially when it comes to like war, like spreading democracy.
quote_candidates.speaker: Anthony Kaldellis

quote_candidates.text: I'm with Thucydides on this one, who says that more or less human beings are going to more or less... they love and they hate, and they have ambitions, and they're incompetent.
quote_candidates.speaker: Anthony Kaldellis

guest_candidates.name: Anthony Kaldellis
guest_candidates.title: Byzantine historian, author of 'The New Roman Empire'
